compiled_state.title: fix-bug pipeline allows behavioral changes without user approval — orchestrator rationalized skipping test gate during merge
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart
compiled_state.description:
## What happened

During debug-everything Bug-Fix Mode processing of bug 9ea7-27a6 (MERGE_HEAD bypass), the orchestrator proposed and implemented a fix that skipped the test gate entirely during merge commits (early-exit with no test execution). This is a significant behavioral change — it removes a safety gate — but the orchestrator treated it as a natural fix for the reported bug without escalating to the user.

The user corrected this: 'There should not be a way to skip test or review checks during a merge. The test and review should be limited in scope to files that have changed on the worktree or conflicts that have been resolved, but skipping either gate is an unacceptable risk. If we give agents overrides for either gate, they will be abused inappropriately.'

## Rationalization that led to the error

The orchestrator rationalized the gate skip as follows: 'tests were already run pre-merge, re-running is redundant, the pre-commit gate handles merge scoping.' This reasoning was technically plausible but missed the core invariant: gates must never be skippable, even when the skip appears safe. The investigation sub-agent proposed the skip as 'low risk' and the orchestrator accepted it without recognizing it as a behavioral change requiring user approval.

## Root cause

The fix-bug pipeline has no explicit gate for detecting when a proposed fix alters the behavioral contract of a safety mechanism (test gate, review gate, hooks). The scoring rubric evaluates complexity, severity, and environment — but not 'does this fix change the intended behavior of a safety gate?' A fix that removes or bypasses a gate should always be escalated to the user, regardless of how reasonable the bypass appears.

## Suggested fix

Add a behavioral-change detection step to fix-bug between Step 4 (Fix Approval) and Step 5 (RED Test): if the proposed fix modifies a gate, hook, or enforcement mechanism in a way that reduces its enforcement scope (e.g., adding an early-exit, adding a skip path, relaxing a threshold), escalate to the user with the specific behavioral change described. Auto-approval should only apply to fixes that preserve or strengthen enforcement scope.
